summary: The paper presents the Phi Modulator Framework and Phi Invariance Conjecture with 105 controlled experiments. The unified framework is a genuine organizational contribution, but the paper suffers from: (1) a massive scope disconnect between the ambitious proposal (Lyapunov/PMP/optimal control) and the actual paper (purely empirical taxonomy + null result), with 4 of 6 proposed theorems absent; (2) missing ImageNet experiments despite being a project constraint; (3) CSI metric with arbitrary weights and zero predictive value consuming significant paper real estate; (4) statistical power too low to support strong claims; (5) the 'conjecture' framing overstates what is essentially a small-scale empirical observation. The paper is publishable as a well-executed negative result with the Phi framework as infrastructure, but needs honest scope reduction, ImageNet data, and removal of non-predictive metric inflation.

findings.description: ImageNet experiments are completely missing. The proposal explicitly planned Phase 4 (ResNet-50/ImageNet, 5 methods x 3 seeds = 15 runs, ~75 GPU-hours). The project constraints mandate ImageNet as a required dataset. The methodology document includes ImageNet as Phase 4. Yet the paper reports ZERO ImageNet results. The paper's own limitations section (6.4.1) acknowledges this as a gap but does not explain why it was not executed. For a paper claiming to characterize 'when dynamic WD matters,' restricting to CIFAR-scale is a fatal weakness for venue credibility at NeurIPS/ICML.
findings.suggestion: Execute ImageNet experiments before submission. If wall-clock time is the constraint, run at least constant + cwd_hard + no_wd (3 methods x 3 seeds = 9 runs) to test whether the Phi Invariance Conjecture holds at scale. If it breaks on ImageNet, that is an equally important finding.

findings.description: Massive scope collapse from proposal to paper. The proposal promises 6 major theoretical contributions: (1) Phi-Modulator Taxonomy with steady-state formula (Theorem 1), (2) Lyapunov Variance Decomposition (Theorem 2), (3) Budget Equivalence Theorem (Theorem 3), (4) PMP Optimal Control Derivation (Theorem 4), (5) Controller Taxonomy of WD methods, (6) AIS threshold characterization. The actual paper delivers ONLY the Phi taxonomy (without the steady-state formula r* = gamma * E[||g||cos(theta)] / (lambda * E[Phi_t])), the three metrics, and the empirical null result. The Lyapunov analysis, PMP derivation, controller taxonomy, and formal budget equivalence theorem are all absent. This is not a minor adjustment—it is a fundamentally different paper than proposed.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) honestly re-scope the proposal to match the delivered paper, removing all references to Lyapunov/PMP/optimal control from the framing, or (b) add at least the steady-state formula and budget equivalence theorem as semi-formal derivations with empirical validation. The current paper is stronger as a clean empirical contribution than as a failed theoretical one.

findings.description: CSI metric has arbitrary weights (w1=0.4, w2=0.3, w3=0.3), combines three dimensionally incompatible quantities (CV of weight norms, log condition number, CV of effective LR), and the paper itself admits CSI does not predict accuracy (rho < 0.3, p > 0.3). The 'sensitivity analysis' testing three nearly-identical weight sets ({0.5,0.25,0.25}, {1/3,1/3,1/3}, {0.4,0.3,0.3}) is insufficient validation—these weights are all roughly equal. CSI occupies significant paper real estate (definition, table, discussion, figure) for zero demonstrated predictive or diagnostic value. This was flagged as a lesson-learned in previous iterations and remains unaddressed.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) remove CSI entirely and redistribute the space to BEM and AIS which do provide interpretable diagnostics, or (b) derive the weights from a principled criterion (e.g., PCA loadings on the three components), or (c) honestly present CSI as an exploratory composite metric whose predictive value remains unvalidated, significantly reducing its prominence.

findings.description: The 'Phi Invariance Conjecture' is overstated. Calling it a 'conjecture' suggests a formal mathematical claim with specified scope and falsification criteria. What the paper actually shows is: on CIFAR-10/100 with ResNet-20 and VGG-16-BN (both with BN), 7 WD methods produce statistically indistinguishable accuracy under AdamW. This is an empirical observation on 2 small datasets with 2 BN architectures, not a conjecture with generality claims. The paper attempts to extend the conjecture to 'sufficiently overparameterized problems with batch normalization' but has no evidence outside CIFAR. The word 'conjecture' in mathematics implies a believed-to-be-true statement; here it is an untested extrapolation from limited data.
findings.suggestion: Reframe as an 'empirical regularity' or 'observed phenomenon' rather than a 'conjecture.' Reserve the conjecture framing for after ImageNet validation confirms the pattern holds at scale. Alternatively, provide explicit falsification criteria for the conjecture.

findings.description: Non-BN ablation (Phase 2 in methodology) is missing from the paper. The methodology planned ResNet-20 without BN to test whether AIS > 0.5 without BN and whether dynamic WD helps—a critical test of the proposed mechanism. The paper's own discussion (Section 6.2) says 'Testing architectures without BN... would further clarify this interaction.' This experiment was planned, is directly relevant to the main claim, and is not present.
findings.suggestion: Execute the non-BN ablation (3 methods x 3 seeds = 9 runs on CIFAR-10). If AIS increases without BN and dynamic WD helps, this validates the mechanism. If AIS stays low and dynamic WD still doesn't help, it still strengthens the paper by ruling out the BN confound.

findings.description: PMP-WD and Hill function experiments (Phase 3 in methodology) are absent from the paper. The proposal's core theoretical contribution was the PMP-derived optimal schedule lambda_t* proportional to gamma_t*(1-delta_t). These experiments were planned (3 methods x 3 seeds x 2 datasets = 18 runs) but not executed. The paper pivoted from a theory-driven paper to a purely empirical one without acknowledging the missing PMP validation.
findings.suggestion: If the scope has been honestly reduced to the empirical Phi framework paper, remove PMP references from proposal.md and alternatives.md. If PMP remains a contribution target, execute the planned experiments.

findings.description: Statistical power is inadequate for the claims made. With N=3 seeds, the paper can only detect effects >= 0.7% (as stated in Section 5.1). The paper claims 'no method produces statistically distinguishable accuracy' but this is a power problem, not a finding. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. The TOST equivalence tests only confirm equivalence at +/-1.0% for 6 of 12 comparisons, meaning the paper cannot even confirm practical equivalence at a tight margin. The paper acknowledges this in Limitations (6.4.4) but the abstract and conclusion do not convey this caveat.
findings.suggestion: Run 9 seeds (as originally planned in the proposal) to achieve power for detecting 0.3% effects. If resource-constrained, at minimum run 5 seeds to reduce the detection threshold. The abstract should explicitly state the power limitation: 'equivalent within our detection threshold of 0.7%.'

findings.description: The abstract claims '105 controlled experiments across 7 methods, 2 optimizers, 2 architectures, 2 datasets, and 3 seeds' but the decomposition in Section 4.2 shows VGG-16-BN was only tested with SGD on CIFAR-10 (21 runs), not with both optimizers and both datasets. The full 2x2x2 grid is not covered. The abstract's framing implies a fully crossed design when it is actually partial. This is misleading.
findings.suggestion: Revise the abstract to accurately describe the experimental coverage: '105 experiments including a full crossed design for ResNet-20 (7 methods x 2 optimizers x 2 datasets x 3 seeds = 84 runs) and a targeted VGG-16-BN validation (7 methods x 1 optimizer x 1 dataset x 3 seeds = 21 runs).'

findings.description: The Phi framework's novelty is organizational, not mathematical. Defining phi(t, theta, g) as a per-parameter modulation function and showing existing methods are special cases is a taxonomy exercise, not a theoretical contribution. The framework does not derive new properties, predict new phenomena, or enable new methods. Its closest analog is the 'kernel view' papers that unify ML methods under a common abstraction. The paper should be honest about this: the contribution is the systematic empirical comparison enabled by the framework, not the framework itself.
findings.suggestion: Reframe the Phi framework as enabling infrastructure for the systematic comparison, not as a standalone theoretical contribution. The real novelty is the empirical finding (Phi Invariance under AdamW) and the diagnostic metrics, not the abstraction layer.

findings.description: The paper title ('The Phi Invariance Conjecture: Why Dynamic Weight Decay Methods Are Equivalent Under AdamW') makes a causal claim ('Why') that the paper does not support. The paper shows THAT methods are equivalent, not WHY. The mechanistic hypothesis (AdamW's adaptive scaling subsumes modulation) is plausible but unproven—there is no theoretical derivation, ablation of AdamW's second-moment estimation, or causal intervention. The paper also does not test whether the equivalence holds for ALL dynamic WD methods (only 7 are tested).
findings.suggestion: Change title to 'The Phi Invariance Conjecture: Dynamic Weight Decay Methods Are Equivalent Under AdamW on CIFAR-Scale Benchmarks' or similar factual framing. Remove 'Why' unless a mechanistic proof is added.

findings.description: VGG-16-BN results (Table 5) show the same Phi Invariance under SGD, which the paper interprets as evidence that BN (not just AdamW) enables invariance. However, the paper does not test VGG-16-BN with AdamW, leaving the interaction between optimizer and normalization untested. A 2x2 design (BN/no-BN x AdamW/SGD) is needed to disentangle the contributions. The current interpretation that 'BN provides an alternative mechanism for Phi Invariance' is one of several possible explanations and is not adequately tested.
findings.suggestion: Add VGG-16-BN + AdamW experiments (7 methods x 3 seeds = 21 runs) and, if possible, a VGG-16 without BN under SGD. This would create the 2x2 design needed to properly attribute the invariance mechanism.

findings.description: Section 5.7 (Cumulative Alignment Analysis) reports non-significant correlations (rho=-0.379, p=0.121 and rho=0.045, p=0.858) then says 'the directional difference (|Delta rho| = 0.424) suggests cumulative alignment may be a more informative measure.' Drawing a directional conclusion from two non-significant results is statistically improper. The difference between two non-significant results is not itself significant without a formal test (e.g., Steiger's z-test for dependent correlations). This was flagged in previous iterations as lesson-learned and remains.
findings.suggestion: Remove the claim about directional difference or add a formal statistical test (Steiger's z-test or bootstrap confidence interval for the difference in correlations). If neither correlation is significant, the honest conclusion is 'neither measure predicts generalization gap in our sample.'

findings.description: The paper references the 'why-weight-decay' codebase (D'Angelo et al., 2024) as infrastructure but does not describe modifications or provide a code repository link. The custom metrics (CSI, AIS, BEM) require specific implementations that are not described in sufficient detail for reproduction. The Hessian condition number approximation via power iteration in CSI is not specified (how many iterations, convergence criterion, which layer's Hessian).
findings.suggestion: Add supplementary material with (a) code repository link, (b) pseudocode for CSI/AIS/BEM computation, (c) implementation details for the Hessian condition number approximation.

findings.description: The related work section (Section 2) cites several papers with only arXiv IDs and no venue information (e.g., 'Ferbach et al., 2026', 'Chen et al., 2026b'). Some citations appear to be from 2026, which may be preprints that could be revised or rejected. The paper should distinguish between peer-reviewed publications and preprints.
findings.suggestion: Mark preprints explicitly as 'arXiv preprint' in citations. For papers cited as ICLR/NeurIPS 2026, verify they have been accepted (the current date is March 2026).

findings.description: The paper uses 7 figures but several are described via alt-text placeholders rather than actual rendered figures. The Figure numbering in text (Figures 2-8) does not match the file references (fig1_taxonomy.png through fig6_sgd_vs_adamw_norms.png). Figure 8 references theorem2_validation.png which carries over from the abandoned Theorem 2 (Lyapunov) framing.
findings.suggestion: Renumber figures consistently. Rename theorem2_validation.png to alignment_validation.png or similar to match the actual content (cumulative alignment analysis, not theorem validation).

metric_gaming_risks: The paper reports 'best test accuracy over 200 epochs' as the primary metric. This is vulnerable to seed selection effects with only 3 seeds—the best epoch can vary significantly across seeds. Final-epoch accuracy should also be reported and compared.

The TOST equivalence tests use margins of +/-0.5% and +/-1.0%, which are generous. In a field where 0.1% improvements are reported, a 1.0% equivalence margin is too lenient. The paper should discuss what margin is practically meaningful.

BEM is computed using expected values of phi across parameters and time. The expectation may mask distributional differences—two methods with BEM=0.5 could have very different temporal or spatial distributions of weight decay, producing different dynamics even at equal budget.
novelty_risks: The Phi framework is essentially a notational abstraction. Defining a general modulation function and showing existing methods are special cases is a standard taxonomy exercise. The real question is whether the abstraction enables new insights—the paper's main insight (Phi Invariance under AdamW) could be discovered without the framework.

The null result (dynamic WD does not beat constant under AdamW on CIFAR) may already be known informally in the community. D'Angelo et al. (2024) showed WD is 'never useful as regularization' which partially subsumes this finding. The paper needs to clearly articulate what is new beyond the D'Angelo observation.

AIS as a metric is essentially Spearman correlation between cosine similarity and loss change—a standard correlation measure applied to standard quantities. Calling it a 'standardized diagnostic metric' overstates the novelty.
reproducibility_gaps: CSI's Hessian condition number component requires power iteration on the full model Hessian. The paper does not specify: (a) is it the loss Hessian or weight-space Hessian? (b) how many power iterations? (c) computed on train or test set? (d) mini-batch or full-batch approximation?

The UnifiedAdamW/UnifiedSGD optimizer classes with pluggable Phi modulator interface are described at a high level but no code is provided. Reimplementing CWD, SWD, etc. within this framework requires non-trivial engineering that could introduce subtle bugs.
The paper does not specify GPU type, training wall-clock time, or whether mixed precision was used. These affect reproducibility particularly for timing-sensitive metrics like CSI.
SWD's gradient-norm sensitivity function h(x) = x/x_bar is specified but the running mean computation (EMA vs. batch mean vs. global mean) is not detailed.
